title: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome: gluteus medius
vignette: A 44-year-old woman comes to the office due to increasing right hip pain for the past several days. The pain is exacerbated by walking and by sitting with the right leg crossed over the left leg. The patient has a history of plantar fasciitis. Vital signs are within normal limits. BMI is 30 kg/m2. On examination, right hip abduction and internal rotation against resistance when the hip is flexed reproduce the pain. The cause of this patient's symptoms is suspected to be pathology at a tendon insertion site. The tendon of which of the following muscles is most likely involved?

clues.note: Lateral hip pain is the presenting complaint. The differential includes tendinopathy (gluteus medius/minimus), bursitis, adductor strain, and referred pain - the maneuvers below localize it.

clues.text: exacerbated by walking and by sitting with the right leg crossed over the left leg
clues.note: Lateral hip pain worsened by walking (loading the abductors) and by leg-crossing (compressing the greater trochanter) is the classic pattern of greater trochanteric pain syndrome - a gluteus medius/minimus insertional tendinopathy.

clues.text: right hip abduction and internal rotation against resistance when the hip is flexed reproduce the pain
clues.note: Pain reproduced by RESISTED hip ABDUCTION (and internal rotation with the hip flexed) stresses the gluteus medius/minimus tendons. Matching the pain-provoking action to the muscle's function is the key to the answer.

clues.text: pathology at a tendon insertion site
clues.note: The stem explicitly localizes the problem to a tendon INSERTION - here, the gluteus medius/minimus insertion on the greater trochanter (insertional tendinopathy), rather than a muscle belly or bursa.

whatMatters: Boil it down: lateral hip pain worse with walking and leg-crossing, reproduced by resisted hip ABDUCTION (and internal rotation when flexed), localized to a tendon INSERTION = greater trochanteric pain syndrome, a gluteus medius/minimus insertional tendinopathy at the greater trochanter. Answer B (gluteus medius).
strategy: For 'which muscle's tendon,' match the PAIN-REPRODUCING maneuver to the muscle's action. Resisted hip ABDUCTION -> gluteus medius/minimus (insert on the greater trochanter). Resisted ADDuction -> adductors. Resisted EXTERNAL rotation -> short external rotators (obturator externus, quadratus femoris). Resisted knee EXTENSION -> quadriceps (vastus lateralis). Map action to muscle.

eliminations.label: adductor brevis - groin pain on resisted ADDuction
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a thigh ADDuctor running from the inferior pubic ramus to the linea aspera. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: adductor strain causes GROIN/medial-thigh pain reproduced by resisted ADDuction, not lateral hip pain on resisted ABDuction. ✅ Correct if: groin pain reproduced by resisted hip adduction.

eliminations.label: obturator externus - external rotator, wrong action
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a short EXTERNAL rotator of the hip inserting at the trochanteric fossa. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: its strain causes pain on resisted EXTERNAL rotation, not the resisted ABDuction/internal-rotation maneuver here, and it is not a lateral greater-trochanter insertional tendinopathy. ✅ Correct if: pain on resisted external rotation.

eliminations.label: quadratus femoris - also an external rotator
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: another short EXTERNAL rotator (ischial tuberosity -> intertrochanteric crest). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: like obturator externus, it is an external-rotation muscle, not the abductor implicated by the pain-provoking maneuver. ✅ Correct if: resisted external-rotation pain.

eliminations.label: vastus lateralis - knee extensor, not a hip abductor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: part of the QUADRICEPS (knee extensor) on the lateral thigh. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: its strain gives anterior thigh pain reproduced by resisted KNEE EXTENSION, not resisted hip abduction at the trochanter. ✅ Correct if: anterior thigh pain on resisted knee extension.

reasoning: Lateral hip pain reproduced by resisted hip abduction/internal rotation
Pathology localized to a tendon insertion at the greater trochanter
Gluteus medius (and minimus) abduct the hip and insert on the greater trochanter
Insertional tendinopathy of the gluteus medius (GTPS) -> answer B
mechanism: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS) is an overuse insertional tendinopathy of the gluteus medius and minimus where they insert on the greater trochanter of the femur (often mislabeled 'trochanteric bursitis'). These muscles abduct the hip, internally rotate it when the hip is flexed, and stabilize the pelvis during gait; resisted abduction/internal rotation stresses the tendons and reproduces pain, and lying or sitting with the leg crossed compresses the trochanter. Risk factors include obesity, altered gait mechanics, and other tendinopathies (eg, plantar fasciitis). Weakness of these abductors can produce a Trendelenburg sign.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid MSK/Anatomy > gluteal muscles and greater trochanteric pain syndrome: gluteus medius/minimus insertion on the greater trochanter, hip abduction, Trendelenburg sign. Cross-ref hip muscle actions.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Convert the exam maneuver into a muscle action: resisted ABDuction -> the abductors (gluteus medius/minimus). Then match the 'tendon insertion at the greater trochanter' clue to gluteus medius.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on greater trochanteric pain syndrome, insertional tendinopathy, hip abduction vs adduction, internal/external rotation, Trendelenburg sign? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The external rotators (obturator externus, quadratus femoris) and the quadriceps (vastus lateralis) are plausible 'hip/thigh' answers, but the resisted-ABDuction maneuver and greater-trochanter insertion specifically implicate gluteus medius.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: An anatomy diagram shows gluteal muscle attachments. Use it to map action to muscle: gluteus medius/minimus insert on the greater trochanter and abduct; the external rotators insert at the trochanteric fossa/crest. Match the provoking maneuver to the insertion site.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Tell me what happened (timing, second-guessing, careless click) and I'll suggest a fix for future questions.

keywords.front: Lateral hip pain + resisted abduction reproduces pain at the greater trochanter
keywords.back: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy)
Hip abduction (+ internal rotation when flexed); insert on greater trochanter; pelvic stabilization
Trendelenburg (pelvis drops to the contralateral side)

firstAid.detail: MSK/Anatomy > gluteus medius/minimus insertional tendinopathy at the greater trochanter; abduction; Trendelenburg sign.
firstAid.location: MSK/Anatomy > gluteus medius/minimus insertional tendinopathy at the greater trochanter; abduction; Trendelenburg sign.

firstAid.detail: Anatomy > adductors (adductor brevis), short external rotators (obturator externus, quadratus femoris), quadriceps (vastus lateralis).
firstAid.location: Anatomy > adductors (adductor brevis), short external rotators (obturator externus, quadratus femoris), quadriceps (vastus lateralis).

firstAid.detail: MSK > insertional tendinopathy/enthesopathy and provocative resisted-movement testing.
firstAid.location: MSK > insertional tendinopathy/enthesopathy and provocative resisted-movement testing.

memoryHook: Lateral hip pain + resisted ABDuction = gluteus medius tendon at the greater trochanter (GTPS).
